name: Bug Bounty Submission Playbook
description: Complete workflow from validated finding to successful bug bounty submission. Covers all major platforms with platform-specific guidance.
author: PocMap AI Bug Bounty Toolkit
trigger_conditions: Vulnerability has been validated with working PoC
prerequisites: Evidence collected (screenshots, HTTP logs, PoC code)

hackerone.tip: HackerOne values detailed technical reports with clear reproduction steps.

bugcrowd.tip: Bugcrowd has a structured form. Fill all fields completely. Markdown attachments are supported.

intigriti.tip: Intigriti values clear, concise reports. European companies prefer formal language.

synack.tip: Synack requires SRT certification. Reports are vetted by leadership before client submission.

phases.name: Pre-Submission Checklist
phases.description: Verify everything is ready before writing the report

steps.action: Double-check the vulnerable asset against program scope
checklist: Domain is explicitly in-scope or matches wildcard?
No pre-approval required for this finding type?
Out-of-scope list doesn't exclude this asset?
steps.failure_action: DO NOT PROCEED. Out-of-scope submissions damage reputation and may violate policy.

required_evidence.description: Burp/ZAP saved request showing payload and vulnerable response
required_evidence.format: Burp state file OR raw text

steps.tips: Create an 'evidence/' folder with numbered files: 01_request.txt, 02_response.txt, 03_screenshot.png

steps.action: Score with official calculator and document justification

cvss_template.AV: Network (N) - The vulnerability is exploitable remotely
cvss_template.S: Unchanged (U) - Scope remains within vulnerable component
cvss_template.C: High (H) - All data on the system can be accessed
cvss_template.I: High (H) - All system files can be modified
cvss_template.A: High (H) - System availability can be impacted

steps.justification_format: For each metric: 'Metric: Value - Brief explanation of why this value applies'

steps.action: Check disclosed reports for similar severity to set expectations
steps.tips: This helps set realistic expectations. NEVER mention expected bounty in the report.
steps.warning: Do NOT write expected bounty amounts in your report. This is considered unprofessional.

phases.name: Report Writing
phases.description: Write the actual bug bounty report

good_examples: Remote Code Execution in Apache Struts 2 (CVE-2017-5638) via Content-Type Header
SQL Injection in WordPress Plugin (CVE-2022-21661) leading to Database Dump
Authentication Bypass in JWT Implementation (CVE-2022-23529) allowing Admin Access

steps.action: Write 2-3 sentence summary for non-technical triagers
steps.template: A vulnerability ({{CVE_ID}}) was identified in {{PRODUCT}} that allows {{ATTACKER_CAPABILITY}}. This could lead to {{IMPACT}}. The vulnerability has a CVSS score of {{CVSS}} and {{EXPLOIT_STATUS}}.
steps.example: A vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228) was identified in the target's Log4j implementation that allows remote code execution without authentication. This could lead to full server compromise and data exfiltration. The vulnerability has a CVSS score of 10.0 and a public exploit is available.
tips: First sentence: What is the vulnerability?
Second sentence: What could an attacker do?
Third sentence: How bad is it (CVSS + exploit status)?
Assume the reader is a product manager, not a security engineer

steps.action: Explain the vulnerability in technical detail
structure: Root cause: Why does this vulnerability exist?
Attack vector: How does an attacker reach it?
Trigger conditions: What input causes the vulnerability?
steps.example: The application uses Apache Struts 2.3.15 (affected by CVE-2017-5638). The Jakarta Multipart parser incorrectly processes Content-Type headers containing OGNL expressions. When a crafted request is sent with a malicious Content-Type header containing OGNL code, the server evaluates the expression, leading to arbitrary code execution.
tips: Show understanding of the underlying code issue
Reference specific functions, parameters, or files if known
Explain WHY it happens, not just WHAT happens
Link to the CVE advisory for technical details

steps.format: Numbered steps, each with: action + expected result
requirements: Each step has exact URL/parameter values
HTTP requests are in code blocks
Expected result stated for each step
A non-technical person could follow these steps

steps.action: Explain the real-world impact of this vulnerability

impact_categories.data_access: What data could be accessed? (PII, credentials, source code)
impact_categories.lateral_movement: Can this lead to compromise of other systems?
impact_categories.user_impact: How many users could be affected?

steps.template: Successful exploitation of this vulnerability allows an attacker to {{PRIMARY_IMPACT}}. Additionally, {{SECONDARY_IMPACTS}}. This affects approximately {{USER_COUNT}} users and could result in {{BUSINESS_CONSEQUENCES}}.
steps.example: Successful exploitation of this vulnerability allows an attacker to execute arbitrary commands on the server with the privileges of the web application user (www-data). Additionally, the attacker could pivot to internal networks, access the database, and exfiltrate sensitive customer data. This affects approximately 500,000 users and could result in regulatory fines under GDPR.
tips: Quantify impact when possible (user count, data volume)
Describe the WORST realistic case, not theoretical maximum
Connect technical impact to business consequences
Mention compliance implications if applicable (GDPR, PCI-DSS, SOC 2)

structure: Alternative fix: If primary isn't immediately feasible
Temporary mitigation: What to do RIGHT NOW
Verification: How to confirm the fix works
steps.template:
**Recommended Fix:**
Upgrade {{PRODUCT}} from version {{AFFECTED_VERSION}} to {{FIXED_VERSION}}. This version patches the vulnerability by {{PATCH_DESCRIPTION}}.

**Alternative Fix:**
If upgrading is not immediately possible, {{ALTERNATIVE}}.

**Temporary Mitigation:**
Until a patch can be applied: {{TEMPORARY_MITIGATION}}.

**References:**
- Vendor Advisory: {{ADVISORY_URL}}
- CVE Details: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/{{CVE_ID}}
steps.example:
Recommended Fix:
Upgrade Apache Struts to version 2.3.32 or 2.5.10.1. This version patches the vulnerability by removing OGNL expression evaluation from the Content-Type header parser.

Alternative Fix:
If upgrading is not immediately possible, disable the Jakarta Multipart parser by setting 'struts.multipart.parser' to 'pell' in struts.properties.

Temporary Mitigation:
Deploy a WAF rule to block requests containing OGNL expressions in the Content-Type header. Monitor logs for exploitation attempts.
tips: Provide a temporary option for immediate risk reduction
Include verification steps so the fix team can confirm

checklist: Executive summary is clear to non-technical readers?
Reproduction steps are numbered and complete?
All URLs and parameters are exact (not 'example.com')?
Evidence attachments are included and accessible?
No expected bounty amount is mentioned?
No spelling/grammar errors in critical sections?

steps.action: Have another researcher review the report
reviewer_questions: Can you reproduce the vulnerability from these steps alone?
Is the impact clear and realistic?
Would this convince you to fix it urgently?

hackerone: Code blocks for HTTP requests and PoC
bugcrowd: Attach PoC as separate file if large
Include Bug URL field with exact vulnerable endpoint

phases.name: Submission
phases.description: Submit the report and track it

after_submit: Note estimated response time from program page

reminder_schedule.day_3: Check if report is being triaged
reminder_schedule.day_7: Polite status inquiry if no response

steps.follow_up_template: Hi team, I'm following up on report #{{ID}} submitted on {{DATE}} regarding {{TITLE}}. I wanted to check if there's any additional information I can provide to help with triage. I'm happy to retest or clarify any points. Thank you for your time!

response_guidelines: Don't be defensive if they ask for clarification
Provide exactly what they ask for - no more, no less
If asked to retest, do it within 48 hours
If you disagree with severity, provide evidence politely

common_requests.retest: Re-run your PoC and confirm it's still reproducible. Document the result.
common_requests.screenshot: Provide additional screenshots of specific behavior.
common_requests.cvss_justification: Expand on why you chose specific CVSS metrics.
common_requests.impact_clarification: Explain the business impact more clearly.
common_requests.scope_verification: Confirm the affected asset is in-scope.

steps.action: Advocate for appropriate severity if downgraded
approach: Reference similar reports with same severity
Be respectful - they have final say
evidence_to_present: CVSS calculator output with your justifications
Similar CVEs with same severity on the platform

steps.request_template: Hi team, now that this vulnerability has been resolved, I'd like to request public disclosure. I'm happy to collaborate on a redacted version if needed. Thank you!

language: Technical terms explained when first used
No ALL CAPS except for emphasis
No emotional language or urgency pressure

common_pitfalls.vague_titles: Use descriptive titles, not 'Found a bug' or 'XSS'
common_pitfalls.missing_evidence: Always include screenshots and HTTP logs
common_pitfalls.unclear_steps: Non-technical person should be able to reproduce
common_pitfalls.wrong_platform_format: Each platform has preferences - adapt accordingly
common_pitfalls.slow_response: Respond to triager questions within 24 hours
